data.title: U-Haul: Needs A Lot Of Work On The Profitability Side Of Things
data.description:
It’s been over 3 years since the first time I covered U-Haul (UHAL), and since then, the company’s share price has lost around 12% in value against SPY’s (SPY) 70% gain, so you would have been better off buying the broader index and sleeping much better than having UHAL in your portfolio. I wanted to go over its performance since then and see whether it is a good time to jump in. Spoiler alert, it is not.

Briefly on Performance

Starting from the top, we can see UHAUL’s revenues took a plunge from mid-’23 to mid-’24 and have been on a decent recovery ever since. The company experienced several weak quarters in 2022-2023, but then self-moving equipment rental finally returned to growth in fiscal 2024-2025. Self-storage has been a steady positive contributor, growing at around 9%-12% per quarter, with a little bit of a deceleration lately. Additionally, U-Haul has been opening and expanding facilities aggressively, with the portfolio reaching over one million storage units across North America, and as of Q3’26, the company added 5.9m net rentable square feet of new storage.

If we take a look at the most recent quarter, Q3’26, we can see that the top-line performance has been mixed: self-moving and equipment barely inching up, while self-storage revenues grew a respectable 7.9%. The rest are too small to list, so here is the overall performance below.

From these numbers, we can see that the growth came from a mix of some improvements in self-moving equipment rental revenues and self-storage revenues, but I would say it is mostly due to the company’s aggressive expansion efforts that drove growth in the top line over the last 1-2 years.

Moving on to the company’s efficiency and profitability, we can see a completely different story compared to its top-line performance. Margins across the board have been on a significant downtrend in the last three years, and it doesn’t look like there is going to be a recovery any time soon. This is because UHAL has a lot of fixed costs, like fleet, interest, labor, and liability, on top of quite modest revenue growth over the last while, with depreciation and weaker gains on truck disposal playing a huge role.

With the aggressive expansion of new locations comes added depreciation expenses, and since new locations usually start at low occupancy, this increases the company’s operating expenses and depresses margins significantly. We can see how much depreciation expenses grew in the latest quarter compared to the same quarter last year, below. An increase of around 20%, and around 32% increase in total.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position as of the latest quarter. UHAL had around $1B in cash and equivalents against $8B in debt. That is a big gap, but is it worrisome? The company is quite capital-intensive due to its real-estate-heavy business, so $8B in debt is not necessarily automatically alarming, but when we see that the company’s operations continue to decline, it could become a lot riskier if the company is not able to cover the interest expenses on the debt. And that is exactly what we see in the latest quarter. Due to the much higher depreciation expense, earnings from operations were a lot lower than at the same time last year ($33.6m vs $150m), and with this amount of debt, the company is paying $95m per quarter in interest expense, making the company unprofitable. It made $37m in net loss on a GAAP basis.

That is not a good outcome for the company. Clearly, operations are not going as well as expected. However, with $1B in cash and $1.3B in cash from operations, I’m sure the company is not going to default any time soon. But it is worth keeping an eye out in case there is further deterioration in operations.

Overall, I am not a fan of where the company’s performance has headed since my first article on it back in 2023, and I am not surprised that its share price underperformed compared to the broader market index.

Comments on the Outlook

So what is the company doing to get out of this rut and turn its operations right back around? It seems that the company is looking to continue to expand its self-storage business going forward, as the management believes this is where significant revenue potential still lies ahead. This, in my opinion, is mostly just hyping up its operations, because I don’t see this happening over the next year. First of all, the opportunity is there, but it relies heavily on modeled projections and ideal occupancy rates. In Q1 ’26, the company said that there is a potential for additional revenue of $261m from non-same-store locations, but the caveat is that this is only achievable if occupancy rates stabilize at around 90%. At that time, occupancy was 66%, and looking at the most recent occupancy was around 72%, down from 78% at the same time last year.

The only way to reach such additional revenue is to improve occupancy rates or expand a lot more aggressively, but expenses are going to increase dramatically as well, which will continue to drive its profitability further down. So, the main issue I see is occupancy rates, not the expansion part. That’s the easy part.

One way the company is trying to increase occupancy rates is through an initiative called 1-Year Price Lock. This means that the customer is not going to be blindsided by frequent price hikes and hidden fees for the first year of renting out the unit. It’s a good idea that is meant to reduce customer churn and improve trust. It must be working since the company’s occupancy rate did improve 600bps from Q1 to Q3. The company is trying to raise occupancy through price discipline, demand targeting, and faster lease-up of new storage capacity instead of relying on discounting, which is never good for profitability.

The company is also leaning a lot more on its migration data to place and grow facilities where population inflows are strongest. This index helps to keep expansion where it matters the most, and I believe there is a lot of opportunity to implement some sort of AI into the tracking of the data to make everything even more efficient. For instance, the Growth Index already uses a large transactional dataset, and with the use of AI, that dataset could be made a lot more powerful if combined with other signals such as housing affordability, local job growth, web traffic, and regional seasonality. Based on these, AI could come up with a more precise forecast model of where to focus its expansion efforts to maximize occupancy and profitability.

Should you buy U-Haul?

I’m afraid I cannot yet recommend the company as a buy. The company’s share price recovered 22% in the last month, and I don’t really see the reason why. Fundamentally, nothing has changed. The company’s operations are still in a downtrend, and it even went negative in the latest quarter, which makes its debt situation a bit worse than before.

I would like to see profitability improving before I would consider the company worthy of an investment; therefore, I maintain my hold rating.

data.title: Warsh, Oil, And The April FOMC: The Case For June Rate Cuts
data.description:
There is a question that's been stuck in my mind since Kevin Warsh was nominated as the next Fed Chair by Trump earlier in January: isn't the market too confident that the Fed has no room to move on monetary policy?

In fact, that question came up many more times as the conflict in the Middle East began, and we are now nearing the April FOMC meeting.

Look, despite being bullish (a stance that I consider contrarian, as I discuss in this piece), I get the concerns. Headline inflation printed a very ugly number in March, oil has spiked, consumer sentiment looks dreadful, and rate cut expectations have been pushed further out. On top of that, there are no signs of the end of the war in the Middle East, even though the market has already priced that in.

However, my view largely differs from the prevailing mood. In fact, I argue that we may see as many as three rate cuts this year, as the data (when looking beyond the war noise) justifies rate cuts as soon as the June FOMC meeting.

I am fully aware that my view (and therefore, my positioning, as I remain fully invested) carries risk, especially after Warsh denied a political bias from Trump to cut interest rates. If the war doesn't come to an end in the next few months and Hormuz remains closed, I may have to reassess my view on rate cuts.

March SEP Embedded Easing This Year

Let’s first separate the noise from the signal.

The March Summary of Economic Projections placed the 2026 median federal funds rate at 3.4%. In other words, they expect one rate cut this year.

As a quick side note, bear in mind that the March SEP was released on March 18, right in the midst of the Middle East conflict, with Brent trading at $107 that day.

In fact, the FED members are expecting further rate cuts next year, with the median federal fund rate projected at 3.1% by the end of 2027.

Let me go back to the Brent oil spike for a moment. Higher oil prices are indeed inflationary. Just take a look at how different the inflation (headline PCE) projections were in December vs March.

However, and this is key, the Fed's favourite metric is core PCE. Please take a look at the expectaions for core PCE between December and March, and again, please bear in mind that the Brent was trading at $107 the day the March SEP was released.

In comparison, the forecast for core PCE was barely changed.

Let me be specific now. In December, the Fed was looking at 2.4% PCE inflation and 2.5% core PCE. By March, the estimates were 2.7% for PCE inflation and 2.7% for core PCE.

Yes, that's still above the Fed's 2% target. However, we haven't seen core PCE trading below the 2% target since the pandemic in 2020. Believe it or not, that's 6 years ago (time flies)!

It is my view that the Fed's 2% target is outdated and needs a revision, as the economy is in a supergrowth cycle. In fact, I wouldn't like to live in an economy where the Fed's 2% target is met simply because it would imply a slowdown in the economy. With the AI supercycled (let's call it a bubble), that ain't happening any time soon, in my view.

Speaking of growth, the same SEP raised the median 2026 real GDP growth projection to 2.4%, up from 2.3% in December. In fact, the 2027 GDP median rose to 2.3% from 2.0%.

With this GDP growth, inflation is unlikely to cool off any time soon.

As for unemployment, the path remained unchanged for 2026, with expectaions still at 4.4%.

The March CPI Report Flipped The Inflation Debate

I still remember the release of the March CPI report, as I began to deploy capital in the markets the day prior to the release. As a side note, I’ve been raising cash since early this year, after I jumped ship from tech during the market broadening.

The headline CPI in March was up a ridiculous 0.9% m/m, the highest since June 2022.

In fact, the energy index was up 10.9% m/m, the largest since September 2005!

I didn't blink at that chart. In fact, my main focus was on core CPI, which excludes the noise (i.e., energy and food).

The increase in core CPI was 0.2% m/m and 2.6% yoy.

In other words, as seen above, the 0.2% m/m increase is basically noise, which suggests that the hike in WTI and Brent has limited contagion beyond energy prices. In fact, shelter increased by only 0.3%, food was 0.0%, and food at home was -0.2%. As a side note, my remark on shelter inflation (i.e., "only") is mainly grounded on the chart below:

The 0.3% m/m change in shelter is noise.

To be precise, the 70 bps gap between core and headline CPI is mainly explained by energy, as gasoline alone accounts for nearly 75% of the headline increase. Interestingly, the monthly change in gasoline prices was the highest ever.

As for gasoline, many experts believe the impact of the increase in crude prices on the CPI will be temporary, with the bulk of it showing up over the next two months. Why?

In my view, it's simply because gasoline prices are behind the jump in headline inflation (75% in March, as I noted above). As seen below, gasoline prices tend to adjust pretty quickly to the WTI.

Therefore, as soon as the conflict in the Middle East is over, I think we may see the WTI down to the low $80s, dragging down gasoline prices and, therefore, headline CPI inflation.

Am I worried about headline inflation? Not really, as the Fed often looks at core (PCE) inflation to reduce the noise from the signal.

The Surveys Are Pessimistic

The title above makes me think that being bullish is a contrarian play. Yes, you read that right.

The latest (preliminary) release of the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index suggests that sentiment is the lowest it has ever been.

Yes, yes, yes. I know the objections to those surveys, as many argue that there is a political bias. I prefer to think (maybe naively) otherwise and believe the survey is a real representation of the consumer sentiment in the U.S. As seen below, the surveys show a dire picture for the U.S. consumer:

That's why I come to the conclusion that being bearish is the obvious trade and being bullish is a contrarian move. I have yet to meet a retail investor who feels pessimistic and still buys U.S. equities.

Coming back to the surveys, the preliminary April University of Michigan year-ahead inflation expectations rose from 3.8% in March to 4.8% in April.

Here is why I think these surveys are mainly noise, even if they correctly represent the consumer expectations.

Take a look at the dotted black line above, representing year-ahead inflation expectations. Notice the spike last year after Liberation Day, followed by a sharp decline in the months after.

Sentiment moves with the current. It can look dramatic on the surface, but it rarely tells you much about the deeper flow.

A Low-Hire, Low-Fire Labor Market

This is important, as it may give Warsh labor-risk justification for rate cuts without recessionary layoffs.

The latest Employment Situation report shows that March nonfarm payrolls increased 178,000, while the combined January-February revision was 7,000 lower than previously reported.

The BLS stated in the report that payroll employment had changed little on net over the prior 12 months. Unemployment is now sitting at 4.3%, which is above the post-pandemic lows of 3.4% in April 2023.

The trend in unemployment over the last few years is on the rise, but it's not recessionary. In fact, in February this year, the JOLTS report showed gross hires at 4.85 million, the lowest since April 2020, and a hiring rate of only 3.1%.

This is not a surprise, given the layoff wave in tech. In my view, the post-pandemic hiring glut, combined with high CapEx plans and the need to show EPS growth, is driving the layoff spree in megacap tech. The latest headlines come from Microsoft and Meta.

In other words, even if the unemployment rate went down in March, I think the underlying trend is dovish, as companies are prioritizing earnings growth over keeping their headcount.

My View On The Interest Rates This Year

The market is expecting no rate cuts for the April FOMC meeting.

That's nothing new. In fact, as I've been arguing many times in my recent articles, the surveys show no rate cuts until next year (now December 2027 appears to be the earliest date for the next cut):

Call me crazy, but I have low conviction in the survey results above. In fact, I think the strongest catalyst this year is a change in perception from no rate cuts until the end of 2027 to two (and even three) cuts.

Here is my rationale:

  1. Fed’s own baseline in the March SEP already allows easing this year, with the median fed funds rate for end 2026 at 3.4%, below the current 3.50%-3.75% range.
  2. The March CPI showed an energy shock, not broad core inflation (i.e., limited contagion outside of gas prices, transportation services or airline fares).
  3. Labor appears to be softer than headline payrolls imply. Even though March payrolls rose 178k and unemployment went down to 4.3%, claims recently rose to 214k, suggesting a low-hire, low-fire labor market.
  4. When (not if) oil prices come down, headline inflation will likely fall quickly, as 75% of the March spike was driven by gasoline prices.

How about Kevin Warsh and the committee hearing this week?

Well, he noted the following remarks on whether he was asked by Trump (who floated rates as low as 1%) on lowering interest rates:

the president never asked me to commit to interest rate cuts ... he did not demand it ... the president never asked me to commit to any such thing nor would I do so

Ok. That clears out a major question on the Fed's independence that we all had in our minds for quite some time.

However, before I conclude this article, I decided to include a short excerpt from this week’s committee hearing below, as food for thought on how independent the next Fed may (or may not) be from the administration:

Warren: Mr. Warsh, did Donald Trump lose the 2020 election?

Warsh: We try to keep politics, if I’m confirmed, out of the Federal Reserve.

Warren: I’m just asking you a factual question. I need to know- I need to measure your independence and your courage.

Warsh: Senator, I believe that this body certified that election many years ago.

Warren: That’s not the question I’m asking. I’m asking, did Donald Trump lose in 2020?

Warsh: I’m suggesting to you in 2020 –

Warren: I’m suggesting you can’t answer that.

Warsh: – the Fed mandate cured your huge inflation problem and you certified the election. We need to keep politics out of monetary policy and monetary policy out of politics.

Ok, so here is the bottom line: I prefer to remain a contrarian (i.e., a bull) and be fully invested heading into the April FOMC meeting. I see two to three rate cuts this year, with the first one likely starting in the June meeting (i.e., Warsh's first FOMC meeting as Chair), as I believe the data warrants cuts this year.
